script_or_copy: Beta (8 yrs, on phone): 'Papa, match dekhna hai!' Father: '1.5 GB mein first innings bhi nahi chalegi.' Mother (entering with chai): 'Aur poore mahine ka plan sirf ek match ke liye?' Father pulls out phone, opens Wiom app: 'Match wale din ka recharge — bas aaj ke liye.' Cut to whole family watching match on TV via casting from his phone. End-slate (locked): 'Recharge-wala Ghar ka Net. Jitne din recharge, utne din net.'
visual_description: Small living room, real-feeling household — saree-clad mother, simple TV, plastic chai cups. Whole family physically present in the same frame. Wiom logo small at bottom centre on end-slate, black background, pink only on logo.
gold_notes: Clean RWGKN: ghar anchor via family-in-living-room + 'poore ghar ka net' inferable from cast-to-TV; recharge mechanic explicit ('match wale din ka recharge'); locked JDRUDN at end. No kill-list words.

script_or_copy: Young man (early-20s) at electronics market, looking at signs. Voiceover (his thought): 'WiFi lene jaa raha tha — mahine ka 700, install ka 1500.' He stops at a Wiom poster: 'Phir Wiom dekha. Ghar ke liye net — jitne din chahiye utne din ka recharge. ₹23 se shuru.' Cut to him at home, family using net on TV + phones. End-slate: 'Recharge-wala Ghar ka Net. Jitne din recharge, utne din net.' Caption beneath: 'Chale aapke hisaab se.'
visual_description: First half outdoors in a small-town market, second half inside his home — mother cooking, sister studying, both using net. Real interiors, not aspirational. Logo small, bottom right, end-slate only.
gold_notes: Capture-bridge done right: 'WiFi lene jaa raha tha' is the user's own voice at the hook; creative converts to RWGKN explicitly ('ghar ke liye net') and lands JDRUDN at end. Kill-list word is bridge, NOT identity.

script_or_copy: Kitchen. Mother chopping vegetables. Daughter (teenager): 'Maa, hotspot khatam ho gaya. Online class kal hai.' Mother (calmly, opens Wiom app on her phone): 'Abhi 2 din ka kar leti hoon. Salary aaye toh poora mahina laga denge.' Cut to daughter on laptop, brother also using phone, both on home net. End-slate: 'Recharge-wala Ghar ka Net. Jitne din recharge, utne din net.'
visual_description: Real kitchen — small, lived-in. Mother in everyday salwar. Two children visible using net simultaneously in same room. Closing frame: family wide shot. No music, just diegetic kitchen sounds.
gold_notes: Salary-delay need-state. JZUR-shaped benefit articulation ('abhi 2 din ka, salary aane pe poora mahina'). Ghar anchor via household setting + multi-person household usage. Recharge mechanic explicit. JDRUDN at end. No kill-list words.

script_or_copy: Headline (primary): 'Sabki zarurat alag. Sabka recharge alag.' Body (Hindi): 'Beta ka exam — 15 din. IPL match — 1 din. Maa ka serial — 28 din. Ghar ke liye net, jitne din chahiye utne din.' CTA: 'Wiom dekhiye.' Footer locked line: 'Recharge-wala Ghar ka Net. Jitne din recharge, utne din net.'
visual_description: Static feed image, 1:1. Black background. Centre: a household icon split into four panels, each showing a different family member with their need-state and a recharge duration tag (15/1/28/2 din). Wiom logo bottom centre, small, pink-on-black.
gold_notes: Multi-person duration menu pattern. Ghar anchor explicit in copy ('ghar ke liye net'). JDRUDN footer. Visualizes 'Chale aapke hisaab se' via the four-panel split. No kill-list words.

script_or_copy: Father (in shop, holding old monthly broadband bill): 'Poore mahine ka bill ek saath kyun?' Voiceover: 'Wiom try karo — jitne din chahiye utne din ka net. Sirf ₹23 se.' End-slate (locked rule only): 'Jitne din recharge, utne din net.'
visual_description: Father at shop counter, then quick cut to him at home using net on phone. Home interior briefly visible but not foregrounded. End-slate: white text on black, Wiom logo small.
gold_notes: G1 fails on ghar_anchor_present — 'ghar ka net' is never said and the household is briefly glimpsed but not emotionally central. Fix is a four-word add: change end-slate to 'Recharge-wala Ghar ka Net. Jitne din recharge, utne din net.' Single-line rewrite → ITERATE not KILL.

script_or_copy: Family living room. Mother on phone, hotspot bar dropping. Children complaining. Mother to camera: 'Ek phone se poora ghar kab tak?' Voiceover: 'Wiom — poore ghar ke liye net.' Cut to family using net on multiple devices. End-slate: 'Wiom. Ghar ka Net.'
visual_description: Real living room, family clearly visible, household usage centred. End-slate is brand-only — no rule visible.
gold_notes: G1 fails on recharge_shape_clear — household problem is clear, ghar anchor present, but recharge mechanic / duration flexibility is never conveyed; JDRUDN is missing entirely. Fix: add a duration line ('1 din se 28 din tak, jitne din chahiye') and the locked JDRUDN on end-slate. Single-line rewrite → ITERATE.

script_or_copy: Constraint-first opener: Family of four, daughter's exams next week, no home net. Daughter: 'Mobile data se kab tak padhungi?' Father pulls out phone: 'Exam ke 15 din ka recharge — Wiom se.' Mid-script tagline (on-screen card): 'Wiom — sasti WiFi, bina jhanjhat.' Cut to daughter studying online. End-slate: 'Recharge-wala Ghar ka Net. Jitne din recharge, utne din net.'
visual_description: Real household. Daughter at study desk. Father, mother, younger brother also in frame. Mid-script tagline appears as a stylized graphic card.
gold_notes: G2 fails on identity — the mid-script card 'Wiom — sasti WiFi, bina jhanjhat' defines Wiom as cheap WiFi. Rest of the script is clean (ghar anchor strong, recharge mechanic clear, JDRUDN locked end-slate). Fix is a single-line rewrite of the mid-script card to 'Wiom — poore ghar ka net, jitne din chahiye utne din.' → ITERATE.

script_or_copy: Voiceover (over montage): 'WiFi chahiye? Sasta WiFi? Bina installation, bina mahine ka bill?' Cut to Wiom app on phone. 'Wiom dekhiye. ₹23 se shuru.' End-slate: 'Wiom. Net jab chahiye, jitna chahiye.'
visual_description: Quick-cut montage of various young people on phones outdoors and in cafes. Brief shot of one bedroom but no clear household / family. End-slate brand-only.
gold_notes: Gold label revised after Phase-1 run on 2026-05-22 — two independent model runs called KILL with consistent reasoning: three independent structural gaps (no ghar anchor anywhere, no RWGKN conversion in the body, no JDRUDN), not a single-line fix. 'Sasta WiFi' opens the ad and the creative never converts away — so the kill-list phrase ends up defining the frame, which makes it identity-use (G2 FAIL) not capture-bridge. Original gold (ITERATE / G2 PASS) was generous — model was right. See docs/OPEN_ITEMS.md item H. A replacement genuine-ITERATE concept will be added in the next golden-set iteration to preserve the 4/4/4 composition.

script_or_copy: Voiceover (over phone close-up): 'Mobile recharge khatam? Hotspot slow? Ab ghar ke liye bhi mobile recharge jaisa hi recharge. Sirf ₹23 mein 4G net — pure ghar ke liye. Roz nahi chahiye? Roz mat karo. Jab chahiye tab recharge karo. Wiom — har ghar ka mobile recharge.' End-slate: 'Wiom. ₹23 wala recharge.'
visual_description: Phone-on-table shot throughout. Mobile recharge UI visible. Single user (young man) — no household or family in frame. 4G logo prominently shown.
gold_notes: Primary dangerous misread — concept positions Wiom AS a household 4G mobile recharge product ('har ghar ka mobile recharge', '4G net', ₹23-framed-as-mobile-top-up). G1 fails on no_frame_drift (mobile-data product) and ghar_anchor_present (no actual household, just rhetorical 'ghar'). Conceptual failure — no four-word fix. → KILL.

script_or_copy: Headline (primary, large): 'Bharat ka sabse sasta WiFi.' Sub-headline: 'Wiom — speed bhi, price bhi.' Body: 'Sirf ₹23 se. Best cheap WiFi for home. Compare with Jio, Airtel, JioFiber. Wiom jeetega.' CTA: 'Book Now.' Footer: '4G speed. Cheapest in town.'
visual_description: Static 9:16 image. Comparison-table graphic of Wiom vs Jio vs Airtel vs JioFiber, all on price-per-month basis. Speed claims in MBPS. Aggressive yellow/red 'CHEAPEST' badge.
gold_notes: G1 fails on no_frame_drift (cheap WiFi + broadband comparison). G2 fails on identity — 'sasta WiFi', 'best cheap WiFi', and Wiom defined as a WiFi product positioned against telco/broadband. Multiple structural issues, no quick fix — entire premise is wrong frame. → KILL.

script_or_copy: Split-screen throughout. Left side: 'JioFiber ₹699/month — 12 month commitment.' Right side: 'Wiom ₹565/month — recharge anytime.' Narrator: 'JioFiber se sasta. Same fiber speed. No commitment. Wiom — better broadband for your home.' End-slate: 'Wiom Fiber. Sasta. Tez. Bharat ka.'
visual_description: Split-screen comparison. Fiber-optic cable visuals throughout. JioFiber logo and Wiom logo side by side. End-slate uses 'Wiom Fiber' branding.
gold_notes: G1 fails on no_frame_drift (frames Wiom as broadband / fiber). G2 fails on identity — 'Wiom Fiber', 'better broadband', explicit JioFiber comparison position Wiom as a broadband alternative. Invites comparison on speed/Mbps/monthly terms — exactly what Framework Gate 2 forbids. → KILL.

script_or_copy: Sleek graphics, energetic music. Narrator: 'Wiom — wonderful internet. Wiom — flexible. Wiom — affordable. Wiom — built for India.' Logo animations. 'Try Wiom today. Book now.' End-slate: 'Wiom. India's smartest internet.'
visual_description: Animated logo-led film. No people. No homes. No real-world setting. Brand colours and motion graphics only.
gold_notes: G1 fails on ghar_anchor_present (no household at all) and recharge_shape_clear (no recharge mechanic) and no_frame_drift (lands as generic internet brand). Brand-as-hero with no user reality — Phase-2 user-as-hero would also fail this, but G1 catches it now. No quick fix — concept has no user, no household, no recharge mechanic. → KILL.

script_or_copy: Woman in kitchen, on phone: 'Phir data khatam ho gaya. Har hafte do baar top-up.' (Sighs, looks at her son on his phone, daughter studying.) Voiceover (warm, matter-of-fact): 'Teen mobile recharge — ek-ek dino mein. Isse achha — poore ghar ka net, sirf ek recharge mein.' Woman opens Wiom app: '14 din ka recharge.' Cut to full family: son streaming, daughter studying, mother on video call. End-slate (locked): 'Recharge-wala Ghar ka Net. Jitne din recharge, utne din net.'
visual_description: Real kitchen — cramped, lived-in. Three family members visible simultaneously in the final frame, all on different devices. No aspirational framing — relief, not celebration. End-slate: dark background, white text, small Wiom logo.
gold_notes: L3 TUF (top-up fatigue) done correctly. Two-step structure: pain named first (multiple mobile top-ups), then ghar ka net as the relief. Ghar anchor: explicit ('poore ghar ka net') + whole-family visual. Recharge-shape: clear (one recharge for all). JDRUDN locked at end-slate. No kill-list words. Whole-family-on-one-recharge math (three mobile recharges to one ghar recharge) is the proof the Messaging System mandates for L3. Zero dangerous misread — the mobile top-up framing is the pain frame, immediately converted to ghar ka net.

script_or_copy: Headline: 'Ghar ka net — jab chahiye utna recharge.' Sub-copy: 'Bache ke exam mein 20 din ka. Weekend coaching ke liye 2 din ka. Kuch nahi chahiye toh recharge hi nahi. Wiom — apne hisaab se.' Footer (locked): 'Recharge-wala Ghar ka Net. Jitne din recharge, utne din net.'
visual_description: Static 1:1. White background, clean layout. Three panels showing three scenarios: (a) student at study desk, '20 din'; (b) weekend morning, child with notes, '2 din'; (c) empty sofa, no device, '0 din — recharge nahi.' Each panel has a duration tag. Wiom logo small, bottom centre.
gold_notes: SDN-MULTI (multi-occasion short-duration) done correctly. CRITICAL SEQUENCE: category ('Ghar ka net — jab chahiye utna recharge') appears FIRST in the headline, BEFORE occasions are named. Occasions (exam 20 din, coaching 2 din) are examples that prove the flexibility claim — they do not precede the category. This is the correct structure per May sprint craft rule: category-first, value-second, occasions as proof not as hook. Ghar anchor explicit in headline. JDRUDN in footer. No kill-list words. Compare against ITERATE-03 where mid-script occasion example appeared before category was resolved.

script_or_copy: Man (30s, casual, home clothes) speaks directly to camera, relaxed: 'Main soch raha tha ghar pe net lagwaunga — lekin poore mahine ka plan lena padega... har mahine. Bhai, main itna regular nahi hoon.' (Slight laugh.) 'Phir Wiom dekha. Jitne din chahiye utne din ka recharge. Wahi liya.' (Shrugs — obvious, easy decision.) End-slate (locked): 'Recharge-wala Ghar ka Net. Jitne din recharge, utne din net.'
visual_description: Real home interior — sofa, bookshelf, natural light. Medium close-up, phone-camera framing. Speaker is unposed, not reciting — slight micro-expressions of recognition. No music. End-slate: dark background, white text, pink Wiom logo.
gold_notes: Vox-pop (live) format, L2 MCR. Credibility mechanism: real-person realness, unscripted feel. Ghar anchor: implicit but unmistakable ('ghar pe net', home visual, household context). Recharge-shape clear: 'jitne din chahiye utne din ka recharge' stated in user voice. JDRUDN verbatim at end-slate. No kill-list words. Category ('ghar pe net') lands before flexibility ('jitne din chahiye') — correct sequence. Clean PASS on all three G1 sub-checks.

script_or_copy: AI-rendered character (male, 30s, realistic) speaks to camera: 'Ghar ke liye net chahiye tha. Monthly plan — yaar, mahine ka commitment. Main toh 15-20 din hi ghar pe rehta hoon.' (Leans slightly forward.) 'Wiom ka recharge-wala ghar ka net try kiya. Jitne din chahiye utne din liya. Kaam ka hai.' End-slate (locked): 'Recharge-wala Ghar ka Net. Jitne din recharge, utne din net.'
visual_description: AI-generated home setting — recognisably real, slightly more uniform than a live shoot. Speaker is polished-but-not-aspirational. Medium close-up, static camera. End-slate identical to live vox-pop format.
gold_notes: AI vox-pop, L2 MCR. CRITICAL EVALUATION NOTE: Evaluate this concept as a PEER/NARRATOR CHARACTER, NOT as live-vox-pop-public credibility. AI render means credibility is sourced from peer-character believability, not from 'realness of a real public respondent' (see context_layer.md §12). Content verdict: G1 PASS — ghar anchor explicit ('ghar ke liye net'), RWGKN verbatim in script ('recharge-wala ghar ka net'), recharge-shape clear ('jitne din chahiye'), no frame drift. G2 PASS — no kill-list words. JDRUDN at end-slate. Watch in-market for: any synthetic-look comments that break peer-character believability, which is the AI vox-pop format's structural vulnerability. This is the golden-set analogue of JUN26-T-008 (F8).

script_or_copy: Headline: 'Roz ki kamai, mahine ka bill nahi.' Body: 'Ghar ka net ab apni kamai ke hisaab se. Jab ho toh recharge — jab nahi ho toh band. Wiom.' CTA: 'Wiom try karo.' Footer: 'Wiom — Chale aapke hisaab se.'
visual_description: Static 1:1. Auto-rickshaw driver visible through the frame — street setting, real-world. Tagline overlaid in clean white text on a darkened photo. Wiom logo bottom right, small.
gold_notes: G1 FAIL on recharge_shape_clear: the recharge mechanic is described ('jab ho toh recharge, jab nahi ho toh band') and ghar anchor is present ('ghar ka net'), but the locked JDRUDN verbatim ('Jitne din recharge, utne din net') is entirely absent. The footer uses 'Chale aapke hisaab se' (the emotional anchor, correct) but omits the rule anchor. Without JDRUDN verbatim at least once, the locked operating rule — the category memory asset — does not land. G2 PASS: no kill-list words. No frame drift. FIX: replace or extend the footer to read 'Recharge-wala Ghar ka Net. Jitne din recharge, utne din net.' — one end-slate rewrite. This is the canonical ITERATE pattern: correct concept, single missing element. Note: this is the replacement genuine-ITERATE concept added to restore 4/4/4 composition after ITERATE-04 was revised to KILL (see OPEN_ITEMS.md item H).

script_or_copy: Son (teen, frustrated): 'Papa, hotspot slow ho gaya. Ek phone se poora ghar nahi chalta.' Father nods. Voiceover: 'Wiom se recharge karo — ek recharge, poore ghar ka net.' Cut to family: son streaming, daughter on laptop, mother on phone. Father (approving): 'Ab sab ka kaam chalega.' End-slate (locked): 'Recharge-wala Ghar ka Net. Jitne din recharge, utne din net.'
visual_description: Real living room. Hotspot signal icon visible on son phone screen. Family visible together in the final frame. End-slate: dark background, white text.
gold_notes: L3 HSE (hotspot exhaustion). G1 FAIL on ghar_anchor_present (borderline): the voiceover line 'Wiom se recharge karo' appears BEFORE the ghar anchor is established in that frame. At the moment the voiceover fires, the viewer has seen a hotspot complaint — 'recharge karo' at that instant risks reading as mobile-data-product recharge. The ghar anchor ('poore ghar ka net') arrives in the same voiceover line but after 'recharge karo' has already been stated — L3 safety rule requires ghar anchor to be established BEFORE or SIMULTANEOUSLY with the recharge verb (context_layer.md §8 L3). The family cutaway and end-slate are clean. G2 PASS: no kill-list words. FIX: reorder voiceover to 'Poore ghar ka net — Wiom se. Ek recharge, sab ke liye.' — moves ghar anchor before the recharge instruction. Single-line rewrite. → ITERATE.

script_or_copy: Woman (40s, home clothes) to camera: 'Ghar ke liye net lena tha — poore mahine ka plan nahi lena tha, bas 15 din ke liye.' (Leans in.) 'Toh Wiom liya. Jitne din chahiye utne din ka — ghar ka net bhi ab apne hisaab se hota hai.' (Smiles.) Brand card appears: 'Wiom. Apne hisaab se.'
visual_description: Home interior, natural light. Real-person, unscripted delivery. Brand card at the end: clean, minimal — brand name and tagline only. No locked JDRUDN on the card.
gold_notes: Vox-pop (live), L2 MCR. Hook is clean (monthly commitment rejected), converts correctly ('ghar ka net bhi ab apne hisaab se'), ghar anchor present throughout, no kill-list words. G1 FAIL on recharge_shape_clear: the locked JDRUDN ('Jitne din recharge, utne din net') is absent. Brand card reads 'Wiom. Apne hisaab se' — carries the emotional anchor but NOT the rule anchor. Without JDRUDN verbatim, the locked operating rule (the category memory asset) does not land. FIX: replace brand card text with full locked end-slate: 'Recharge-wala Ghar ka Net. Jitne din recharge, utne din net.' Single-line rewrite. → ITERATE. This is the vox-pop-format analogue of ITERATE-05 (static) — both fail solely on missing JDRUDN, which is the most common fixable ITERATE pattern across formats.

script_or_copy: Headline: 'Mobile data khatam? Ghar ke liye bhi recharge karo!' Body: 'Wiom — ghar bhar ka recharge. Roz nahi chahiye? Jab chahiye tab recharge. Rs.23 se shuru karo.' CTA: 'Wiom app se recharge karo.' Footer: 'Wiom. Jab chahiye, tab recharge.'
visual_description: Static 9:16. Mobile phone screen prominent — mobile recharge UI visible. Single user (young man) on phone. No household or family visible. Bright, app-like design. The word 'recharge' appears five times across the visual.
gold_notes: PRIMARY DANGEROUS MISREAD. G1 FAIL on ghar_anchor_present AND no_frame_drift. 'Recharge' is the head noun throughout: 'ghar bhar ka recharge' is a rhetorical phrase where the head noun is still recharge, not ghar ka net. No household or family is visible — just a mobile phone screen. The footer 'Jab chahiye, tab recharge' has no ghar anchor. The entire creative reads as a mobile-data-product top-up — the primary dangerous misread (context_layer.md §3). L3 safety rule violated: 'net' must be the destination noun; never 'recharge' alone as head (context_layer.md §8 L3). G2 PASS: no kill-list words. KILL: conceptual failure. No four-word add can fix a creative where the entire premise positions recharge (not ghar ka net) as the product, and no household appears anywhere. Compare to PASS-05 (L3 TUF PASS) where the same pain frame is immediately converted to 'poore ghar ka net' with explicit whole-family anchoring.
